card.intuition: Visit the current node BEFORE its children — Root, Left, Right. You process a node the moment you first touch it.
card.analogy: Reading a table of contents before the chapters: you record each chapter title when you encounter it, then dive into its sub-sections.
card.space_complexity: O(h) where h is the tree height — O(log n) balanced, O(n) skewed
when_to_use: Copy or serialize a tree — pre-order captures the parent before children, making reconstruction possible
Finding a path from root to a target node
Building a prefix-based trie from keys
gotchas: Iterative pre-order with a stack: push right child before left so left is processed first (stack is LIFO)
Pre-order visits root first — don't confuse with in-order (Left, Root, Right) which gives sorted output for BSTs
Recursive and iterative produce the same order; use iterative for very deep trees to avoid stack overflow

exercise.prompt: Implement iterative pre-order traversal using an explicit stack.
hints: Push right child first — since stack is LIFO, right will be processed AFTER left, which is correct for Root→Left→Right order
Then push left child — it will be popped next, maintaining pre-order sequence
exercise.test_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self,v,l=None,r=None): self.val=v;self.left=l;self.right=r
root=TreeNode(1,TreeNode(2,TreeNode(4),TreeNode(5)),TreeNode(3))
assert preorder(root) == [1,2,4,5,3], 'Standard pre-order'
assert preorder(TreeNode(1)) == [1], 'Single node'
assert preorder(None) == [], 'Empty'
root2=TreeNode(1,TreeNode(2,TreeNode(3)))
assert preorder(root2) == [1,2,3], 'Left-skewed'
print('PASS')
